stem: In triangle \( ABC \), \( \angle A = [[a]]^{\circ} \) and \( \angle B = [[b]]^{\circ} \). What is the measure, in degrees, of the exterior angle of the triangle at vertex \( C \)?
solution: By the exterior angle theorem, an exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two remote interior angles. The exterior angle at \( C \) has remote interior angles \( A \) and \( B \), so it measures \( [[a]] + [[b]] = [[answer]] \) degrees.
distractor_rationales: This is the interior angle at \( C \), which is the supplement of the exterior angle, not the exterior angle itself.
This subtracts only \( \angle A \) from \( 180^{\circ} \), treating the two given angles as supplementary to the answer.
This subtracts only \( \angle B \) from \( 180^{\circ} \).
This is off from the correct value by ten degrees.
This is off from the correct value by ten degrees.
This is off from the correct value by twenty degrees.

stem: Three rays from a point \( P \) divide the full angle around \( P \) into three non-overlapping angles measuring \( [[a]]^{\circ} \), \( [[b]]^{\circ} \), and \( x^{\circ} \). What is \( x \)?
solution: The angles around a point sum to \( 360^{\circ} \). So \( x = 360 - [[a]] - [[b]] = [[answer]] \) degrees.
distractor_rationales: This uses \( 180^{\circ} \) (a straight angle) instead of the full \( 360^{\circ} \) around a point.
This just adds the two given angles instead of subtracting them from \( 360^{\circ} \).
This subtracts only \( \angle a \) from \( 360^{\circ} \), ignoring \( \angle b \).
This subtracts only \( \angle b \) from \( 360^{\circ} \), ignoring \( \angle a \).
This is off from the correct value by ten degrees.
This is off from the correct value by ten degrees.

stem: Two angles form a linear pair (they are supplementary and adjacent). One angle exceeds three times the other by \( [[k]] \) degrees. What is the measure, in degrees, of the smaller angle?
solution: Let the smaller angle be \( x \). The larger angle is \( 3x + [[k]] \), and since the pair is supplementary, \( x + (3x + [[k]]) = 180 \). Solving, \( 4x = 180 - [[k]] \), so \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the larger angle, \( 3x + [[k]] \), not the smaller one.
This divides \( 180 - [[k]] \) by 3 instead of by 4, forgetting the smaller angle's own coefficient of 1.
This subtracts the correct answer from \( 180 \), effectively re-deriving the larger angle by a wrong route.
This adds \( [[k]] \) to the correct smaller angle instead of solving the full equation.
This is off from the correct value by five degrees.
This is off from the correct value by five degrees.

stem: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. The two same-side interior (co-interior) angles measure \( ([[a]]x + [[b]])^{\circ} \) and \( ([[c]]x + [[d]])^{\circ} \). What is the value of \( x \)?
solution: Same-side interior angles formed by a transversal across parallel lines are supplementary, so \( ([[a]]x+[[b]]) + ([[c]]x+[[d]]) = 180 \). Combining like terms and solving gives \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the coefficients of \( x \) instead of adding them, as if the angles were equal rather than supplementary.
This mishandles the sign on \( d \), effectively treating the angles as if they were being set equal instead of summed to \( 180 \).
This is off from the correct value by one.
This is off from the correct value by one.
This is off from the correct value by two.
This doubles the correct value of \( x \).

stem: An isosceles triangle has a vertex angle (the angle between the two equal sides) measuring \( [[v]]^{\circ} \). What is the measure, in degrees, of each base angle?
solution: The two base angles of an isosceles triangle are equal, and all three angles sum to \( 180^{\circ} \). So each base angle is \( \frac{180 - [[v]]}{2} = [[answer]] \) degrees.
distractor_rationales: This is the sum of the two base angles together, not a single base angle.
This mistakenly halves the vertex angle instead of the remaining angle sum.
This subtracts twice the vertex angle from \( 180^{\circ} \) instead of subtracting once and halving.
This divides the remaining angle sum by 3 instead of 2, as if there were three equal angles.
This is off from the correct value by five degrees.
This is off from the correct value by five degrees.
